summary: Round 2: deeper/fresh-angle battery of the Kamikakushi PRD (exploits, economy sim, onboarding, failure-states, build diversity, cross-doc, content-volume, devils-advocate); dedup vs round 1; verify; synthesize new decisions
logs: Round 2: launching 13 fresh-angle auditors (dedup vs round 1)...

confirmedFindings.title: The market/economy numbers (koku↔coin rate, buy-side prices, saturation) are promised in §4 but never defined — an unbounded craft/arbitrage money loop cannot be ruled out
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.4(c) line 974 ("numbers → §4"); §2.4(b) line 966 (sell-side only); §4.7 lines 2827-2911 (no market table); §4.7.2 lines 2872-2874 (craft value step)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.4(c) line 974 defines `MarketState { perGoodPriceIndex, saturationByGood, recoveryRate }` and promises "**(numbers → §4)**" — but §4.7 (the producer/cost section) defines gathering yields, crafting, loot, and building costs and contains NO koku↔coin exchange rate, NO buy-side prices, and NO bid-ask spread. The saturation damper is sell-side ONLY (§2.4b line 966: "depresses **sell** prices when one good is flooded"). Meanwhile crafting CREATES value: §4.7.2 lines 2872-2874, "Each quality tier multiplies the item's stat/value by **`1.25^tier`**" (tier-5 ≈ ×3.05) with only "~60%" disassembly return. Without a defined buy price or spread, gather-cheap-materials → craft-high-quality → sell-high has no specified floor preventing an unbounded coin/koku loop (dodge per-good saturation by cycling goods or waiting for recovery). Via finding exploit-degenerate-1, that unbounded money then launders into the uncapped Estate TREASURY/LAND deeds and the Estate gate.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The exploit lens cannot even audit for an infinite-money loop because the core trade numbers don't exist where the PRD says they do — a dangling cross-ref AND a balance gap. Given finding #1, unbounded money is not a harmless number-go-up: it feeds the Estate Influence gate.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add the §4 market table the §2.4(c) reference promises: koku↔coin exchange rate, per-good buy prices (or a buy/sell spread), and the saturation/recovery numerics — sized so a gather→craft→sell round-trip cannot be a strictly-positive infinite loop (or so that any positive margin is bounded by labour time at the per-rank floor).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Factual core verified: §2.4(c) line 974 (`MarketState {perGoodPriceIndex, saturationByGood, recoveryRate}` "(numbers → §4)") and §3.8 line 2188 ("market-saturation rate ... deferred [to §4]") both promise market numerics in §4, yet §4 contains none — §4.7 (lines 2827-2911) defines yields/crafting/loot/building costs but no koku↔coin exchange rate, no buy-side prices, and no bid-ask spread; a full-doc grep confirms no exchange rate exists anywhere. The crafting `1.25^tier` value step with ~60% disassembly return is real (line 2873). So the dangling cross-reference + missing market table is a genuine completeness gap. HOWEVER the finding's headline exploit framing ("unbounded craft/arbitrage money loop ... feeds the Estate gate") is overstated and largely refuted by structural guards already in the PRD: the trade ≤⅓ HARD INVARIANT (§4.2.3, verifier-enforced §6.6, "canon and NOT tunable") clamps the TRADE strand so trade "can never carry a tier gate," and the saturation damper is explicitly designed to "stop trade running away" (§2.4b line 967). The exploit "cannot be ruled out" only because numbers are deliberately deferred — true of nearly every "(proposed)" balance value in the doc — not because a positive-margin loop is demonstrated. Net: real but LOW-severity documentation/balance gap (fill in the §4 market numbers the two sections point to), not a confirmed degenerate economy. Genuinely new vs the trade-cap material (which is present and intact). fix_class needs-human because choosing the exchange rate, buy prices, spread, and saturation/recovery numerics is balance/design judgement, not clerical.
confirmedFindings.question: §2.4(c) and §3.8 both defer the market numerics (koku↔coin exchange rate, per-good buy/sell prices or spread, saturation/recovery rates) to §4, but §4.7 never defines them. What are the intended values, and should §4 add a short market-pricing subsection? Note the unbounded-loop risk is already structurally bounded by the trade ≤⅓ HARD INVARIANT (§4.2.3) and the saturation damper, so this is a completeness gap rather than a confirmed exploit — but the buy-side/spread numbers should still be sized so a gather→craft→sell round-trip's margin is bounded by labour time at the per-rank floor.

confirmedFindings.title: "Soft stamina never to zero" has no realizing floor constant — the low-satiety rate can asymptote to a halt
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.3(c) L940-941 and §4.7.1 L2837 (and the whole of §4 — no constant exists)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.3(c) L940-941: "a soft-throttle function maps low satiety → a *rate multiplier* on labour/combat speed (never to zero)." The only place the multiplier appears in the balance section is §4.7.1 L2837: "yieldPerAction = baseYield · (1 + 0.04·skillLevel) · toolMult · seasonMult · (soft-stamina rate)". No SATIETY/STAMINA floor value, curve, or minimum multiplier is defined anywhere in §4 (the throughput tie-out at L2855 only hand-waves "(after stamina, food, and re-investment)").
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: "Never to zero" is the canonical guarantee that low satiety only PACES play, never stalls it. But there is no number that enforces a floor. A literal "rate falls as satiety falls" implementation can drive the multiplier arbitrarily close to 0 — i.e. the "slow to a halt" the soft system was explicitly designed to forbid. This is exactly the boundary the soft-stamina contract must pin down before build.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Define a STAMINA_RATE_FLOOR constant (e.g. low-satiety actions run at no slower than 0.25–0.5×) and the satiety→rate curve in §4, and reference it from the §4.7.1 formula.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed accurate and genuinely a gap. §2.3(c) L940-941 states the soft-throttle maps low satiety to a rate multiplier "(never to zero)" — a qualitative contract only. §4.7.1 L2837 uses "(soft-stamina rate)" as a load-bearing factor in the core yieldPerAction formula. §3.8 L2188 explicitly defers "soft-stamina rate" to §4. Yet §4 never defines the satiety->rate curve or a minimum-multiplier floor, and — decisively — the §4.9 "Levers index (the tuning dashboard)," which enumerates every tunable constant (DAMAGE_FLOOR, XP_BASE, PRODUCER_GROWTH, season dials, etc.), omits soft-stamina rate entirely. So the headline "never to zero" guarantee has no realizing number anywhere, even in the master lever list. A naive satiety/satietyMax implementation would hit 0 at empty, violating the contract. This is a real design/balance gap with a genuine trade-off (high floor = cosmetic satiety; low floor = the crawl-to-halt the soft system explicitly forbids), so it requires a human decision rather than a clerical fix. Appears new (round-1 fixes were applied; soft-stamina floor is not an obvious round-1 topic).</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Define the soft-stamina/satiety -> rate-multiplier function in §4 and add it to the §4.9 levers index: (1) What is STAMINA_RATE_FLOOR — the minimum labour/combat speed multiplier at zero satiety that realizes the "never to zero" contract (proposed range ~0.25-0.5x)? (2) What is the curve shape from full satiety (1.0x) to empty (floor) — linear, or flat-then-knee so only deep depletion bites? Trade-off: a high floor (e.g. 0.6x+) makes satiety nearly cosmetic and removes the pacing nudge that gives food/cooking purpose (§2.3 a/b); a low floor (approaching 0) risks the "crawl to a halt" the soft system was explicitly designed to forbid. The chosen floor must also be wired into the §4.7.1 yieldPerAction formula and listed in the §4.9 tuning dashboard alongside the other floor constants.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: What is the minimum soft-stamina rate multiplier (the floor that realizes "never to zero") and the shape of the satiety→rate curve from full to empty? Trade-off: a high floor makes satiety nearly cosmetic; a low floor risks the "crawl to a halt" the soft system forbids.

confirmedFindings.title: Combat has three player exits (win / loss / retreat) but only win and loss are specified — retreat's resolution is undefined
confirmedFindings.evidence: Retreat is a first-class verb: §2.8(a) L1097 "the player intervenes with stance / ability / item / **retreat**"; §6.3 L3993 "{ type: 'combat_action'; action: CombatAction } // ability/item/**retreat**". But §4.6.5 defines only the on-kill (win) path and §4.6.6 is titled "the soft-setback-on-**loss** rule" — there is no §4 rule for what a retreat does (clock cost? HP carried out? loot kept/dropped? does it count as a loss / fire an Arms dent? where does it leave the MC?).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Retreat is the player's intended escape valve (and the thing that should prevent the loss-spiral in failure-states-2), yet its outcome is completely unspecified. A builder cannot wire the retreat intent without inventing semantics, and getting it wrong (e.g. treating retreat as a loss-with-dent) silently violates the soft-setback intent.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a §4.6 sub-rule for retreat: its clock cost, whether HP/injury/loot carry, that it never dents Influence, and the area it returns the MC to.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against docs/prd.md. Retreat is a first-class combat-intervention verb in §2.8(a) L1097, §2.8(b) L1110, §2.8(c) L1123 (CombatInterventionIntent), §4.6 L2751, and §6.3 L3993 (combat_action intent). But the §4.6 resolution rules cover only two of the three exits: §4.6.5 L2796 is the on-kill (win) path, and §4.6.6 L2804/L2815 is explicitly the soft-setback-on-LOSS rule (1 HP, ~half-day clock, light injury, possible carried-loot drop, possible scripted Arms dent on a defence-deed). There is no rule for what a retreat resolves to — clock cost, whether HP/injury/loot carry out, whether it counts as a loss or fires an Arms dent, and where it leaves the MC. Confirmed nothing in prd_human_feedback.md or brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md defines retreat's outcome; the locked-decisions doc (L105) only lists it as an intervention verb. A builder cannot wire the retreat intent without inventing semantics, and mis-wiring it (e.g. routing it through §4.6.6 as a loss-with-dent) would silently violate the soft-setback/escape-valve intent. The fix requires design judgement on multiple trade-offs (does retreat cost the full ~half-day or less? does it ever dent Arms? does it drop loot?), so it is needs-human, not a clerical apply.
confirmedFindings.question: What does a successful retreat resolve to mechanically, and how does it differ from a loss (§4.6.6)? Specifically: (1) Clock cost — full ~half-day recovery like a loss, a smaller fixed cost, or none? (2) Does HP carry out as-is (no drop to 1 HP), and is there any injury risk? (3) Loot — keep all carried loot, or still roll the drop chance? (4) Does retreat ever count as a loss for the purposes of the Arms dent — e.g. retreating from a *defence-deed* fight — or is retreat guaranteed to NEVER dent Influence? (5) Where does the MC return — the area entrance / safe hub, and is the fight re-attemptable? Recommended framing: retreat as a clean escape valve (HP/loot/levels all carried, a modest clock cost, never an Influence dent) so it genuinely prevents the loss-spiral; the main open trade-off is whether abandoning a defence-deed mid-fight should still register as a failed defence and dent Arms. Add this as a §4.6 sub-rule (e.g. §4.6.7 retreat) parallel to §4.6.5/§4.6.6.

confirmedFindings.title: Resources: §6.5 says resources carry "caps" but the ResourceDef shape has none and §6.4 stores uncapped counts — overflow/hard-cap behavior is undefined
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.5 registry table L4090 vs §2.4(c) ResourceDef L970-971 vs §6.4 GameState L4046 / L4051
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.5 L4090: "| `content/resources.ts` | resources (koku, coin, wood, fish, materials…) + display/emoji + **caps** | `ResourceId` |". But §2.4(c) L970-971 ResourceDef enumerates its fields with no cap: "`ResourceDef { id, name, kind (...), revealPredicate, stackable, perishable?, spoilTicks? }`". And §6.4 stores them uncapped: L4046 "resources: Record<ResourceId, number>", L4051 "inventory: Record<ItemId, number>". No §4 number defines any cap value, and no rule defines what happens at a cap (block acquisition vs. discard overflow).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: "Inventory overflow or a hard cap?" is a boundary the economy must define. If resources/inventory are capped, the cap field is missing from ResourceDef and the magnitudes/overflow behavior are unspecified; if they are not, §6.5's "caps" is a stale claim. An exponential currency (koku) silently hitting an undefined cap, or an item stack overflowing with undefined disposition, is a build-time failure state.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either add a `cap?` field to ResourceDef (§2.4c) + cap values in §4 + explicit overflow behavior, or strike "+ caps" from §6.5 and state resources/inventory are unbounded.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against source. §6.5 registry table L4090 lists `content/resources.ts` as holding "...display/emoji + caps | ResourceId". But §2.4(c) L970-971 enumerates ResourceDef fields with no cap: `ResourceDef { id, name, kind, revealPredicate, stackable, perishable?, spoilTicks? }`, and §6.4 stores resources/inventory as plain uncapped counts (L4046 `resources: Record<ResourceId, number>`, L4051 `inventory: Record<ItemId, number>`). A full grep for cap/overflow/stack/capacity/maxStack confirms NO resource-quantity cap value or overflow rule exists anywhere in the PRD — every "cap" is the per-event accrual cap, the trade ≤⅓ structural cap, soft-cap XP curves, or STR hauling capacity. So "+ caps" in §6.5 is an unbacked outlier against an otherwise consistently-uncapped, counts-only design. The inconsistency is genuine and appears new (round-1 findings did not touch the resources registry caps wording). Severity downgraded medium→low: the discrepancy is a single stray word in a registry table with no downstream wiring; the weight of the spec (data shape, GameState storage, absence of any cap magnitude) consistently treats resources as unbounded. Fix is needs-human, not trivial-apply: the word "caps" is ambiguous (hard quantity cap vs. display-formatting cap) and choosing to strike it asserts "resources are unbounded" — a (minor) economy design statement, and an exponential currency (koku) makes the no-cap reading likely but not certain.
confirmedFindings.question: Do resources (and inventory) have hard quantity caps, or is "+ caps" in §6.5's resources.ts row a stray/mis-meant word? Options: (A) No caps — strike "+ caps" from §6.5 L4090 and optionally note in §2.4(c) that koku/coin/materials/inventory are unbounded counts (consistent with exponential-koku canon and the counts-only GameState). (B) Caps exist — add a `cap?` field to ResourceDef (§2.4c) [and/or ItemDef for inventory], define cap magnitudes in §4, and specify overflow behavior (block-on-acquire vs. discard overflow). (C) "caps" meant display caps (digit/number formatting) — reword to "display caps" and keep quantities uncapped. The economy boundary must pick one; recommend (A) unless storage/warehouse limits are an intended mechanic.

confirmedFindings.title: Two surfaces unlocking on the same reduce/tick has no defined serialization — the signature "one reveal at a time" pacing is undefined at simultaneity
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.1(a) L862-865 frames each reveal as a single beat ("one event that simultaneously: pushes a diegetic log line, reveals the next panel/tab/resource/area...") and §2.1(b) as fractal/one-at-a-time ("things *appear*, each announced in-fiction"). But §6.3 reduce L4009-4011: "re-checks unlock and tier-threshold predicates so **newly-earned surfaces** flip to unlocked and push their diegetic log line" — plural surfaces can flip true in a single reduce/tick (e.g. one deed crossing a pillar threshold AND satisfying a rank gate). No queue/ordering is specified.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: When ≥2 predicates become true on the same tick, multiple panels reveal and multiple log lines stack at once — undermining the signature "act → ONE thing fades in with a log line" pacing the whole presentation is built on. Whether reveals are serialized (one per beat) and in what order is an edge the reveal engine must define.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify a reveal queue: simultaneously-earned surfaces drain one-per-beat (or per-tick) in a deterministic order, so the one-at-a-time cadence and log readability hold.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed the quoted text. §2.1a (L862-865) and §1.12 (L692-695) frame each reveal as "one event," and §6.3 reduce (L4009-4011) says "newly-earned surfaces flip to unlocked and push their diegetic log line" — plural, with no ordering. The §3.0 "Rows are in reveal order" (L1749) is authoring-table order WITHIN one ladder; the fractal "one piece at a time" rule (L1755) governs reveals spread across multiple actions, not a single tick. None of these defines the runtime behavior when ≥2 predicates (e.g. a PILLAR threshold and a RANK rung, distinct trigger kinds per L1741-1746) become true on the same reduce/tick. So the simultaneity case — whether multiple panels/log-lines fire at once or drain one-per-beat, and in what cross-track order — is genuinely undefined. It is a real low-severity gap against the signature "act → ONE thing fades in" pacing the whole presentation is built on. It is distinct (engine-level simultaneity/serialization), not a restatement of authoring-order content. Fix is needs-human: choosing serialize-vs-simultaneous and a deterministic ordering is a UX/pacing design call, not a clerical edit.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">When two or more reveal predicates flip true on the same reduce/tick (e.g. a single combat deed crosses a PILLAR threshold AND a RANK rung, or a quest's flagsSet satisfies several STORY gates at once), how should the reveal engine present them to preserve the signature "act → ONE thing fades in with a log line" cadence? Options: (A) Serialize into a one-per-beat reveal queue that drains over subsequent frames/ticks in a deterministic order (e.g. registry/reveal-order index, then trigger-kind priority RANK > PILLAR > STORY > FIRST-USE), keeping each beat readable but delaying some reveals; or (B) allow a simultaneous multi-reveal in one beat (multiple log lines stack in the capped feed, multiple panels fade in together), which is simpler and keeps reveals atomic with the action but can stack 2-3 panels/lines at once and dilute the one-at-a-time feel. If (A), the deterministic drain order must be specified so replay/determinism (§6.3 guarantee) holds. Trade-off: pacing/readability and determinism-of-ordering (A) vs implementation simplicity and atomic act-to-reveal coupling (B).</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: When two or more surfaces unlock on the same tick, should reveals be serialized into a one-per-beat queue (and in what deterministic order), or is a simultaneous multi-reveal acceptable for the signature reveal pacing?

confirmedFindings.title: Coin (mon) is a dead-end currency: canon T1 currency with sources but no priced sink, no koku↔coin exchange rate, and a market engine with zero numbers
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.4(b)(c)(e) L963-984; §4.7.5 L2900-2911; §3.8 L2187-2188; §4 (L2265-3100, grep-confirmed: coin appears only as flavour)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.4(b) L963-967: "convert surplus to coin via trade (brokers/shops); spend koku/coin/materials on crafting, gear, building, and tier-expansion … A market-saturation damper (2.11/2.15) depresses sell prices". §2.4(c) L974: "MarketState { perGoodPriceIndex, saturationByGood, recoveryRate } (numbers → §4)." §2.4(e) L983-984: "Coin (mon) reveals at T1 when the village market/shop row opens." §3.8 L2187-2188 defers "market-saturation rate" to §4. But §4.7.5's only priced sinks are koku+materials (E1 "400 koku | 30 wood", E2 "2K koku | 120 wood, 40 stone") — zero coin; §4.7.2 crafting costs are material bundles; and grep of all of §4 finds 'coin' only as flavour ('market/coin opens … coin row lit', L2987). No koku↔coin exchange rate, no shop price, and no saturation/price-index number exists anywhere. §2.17 BuildableStructure lists costs '(koku/coin/materials/labour)' (L1538) but §4.7.5 never assigns a coin cost. Coin is gathered (trade surplus) and rewarded (loot drops coin, §2.10b; bandits 'Drop worn weapons … + coin', L3395) but is never spendable in the numbers.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Coin is a canon currency whose introduction IS the headline reveal of T1-V0 ('coin row lit'), and the whole T1 trade economy (broker meters, the silk meibutsu, the market-saturation damper, the ≤⅓ Estate cap on raw trade yields) hangs off it. With no exchange rate and no priced sink, the player accumulates an unspendable currency and the T1 market literally cannot be implemented or balanced — the §4 'number spine' silently omits an entire canon subsystem it twice promises ('numbers → §4').
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Define, in §4: a koku↔coin sell/buy spread (the broker conversion), at least one coin-denominated sink (shop goods / gear / repair / a coin component of building costs), and the MarketState numbers (base price index per good, saturation increment per sale, recovery rate/day, and how shop standing softens the crash). Until then mark coin/market as a known v1 hole, not a frozen spec.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against source. §2.4(c) L974 defines MarketState{perGoodPriceIndex, saturationByGood, recoveryRate} with an explicit "(numbers → §4)" promise, and §3.8 L2188 defers the "market-saturation rate" to §4. But §4 contains NONE of these: a full grep of §4 (L2265–3101) finds 'coin' only as flavour (unit gloss L2249, "coin opens" L2987), no price index, no saturation increment, no recovery rate, and no koku↔coin exchange rate. §4.7.5 building costs (E1 400 koku/30 wood; E2 2K koku/120 wood,40 stone) and §4.7.2 crafting (material bundles) assign ZERO coin sinks, even though §2.17 L1538 BuildableStructure lists coin as a cost component and §2.4(b) L963-964 says players "spend koku/coin/materials on crafting, gear, building." So coin is gathered (trade surplus L963, loot L1186, bandit drops L3395, RewardBundle.coin L879) but unspendable in the numbers — and the market engine that the T1 trade economy hangs off has no balance numbers despite two "numbers → §4" cross-refs. Coin is in v1 scope (reveals at T1-V0, the headline 'coin row lit' beat L2987; v1 ships T0–T2; market is M4 L4593). Genuinely new vs the round-1 fixes already applied (F1 trade self-reference, T0 koku-band reconciliation — both unrelated). needs-human because defining an exchange rate, a coin-denominated sink, and the saturation/price-index numbers are all balance/design decisions with real trade-offs.
confirmedFindings.question: The §4 number spine omits the entire coin/market subsystem despite §2.4(c) and §3.8 twice promising "numbers → §4". To make coin spendable and the T1 market buildable/balanceable in v1 (M4), §4 needs: (a) a koku↔coin sell/buy spread (the broker conversion rate, since coin is only obtained by converting koku-surplus or as loot); (b) at least one coin-denominated sink — options: shop goods/gear prices, repair costs, or a coin component added to E1/E2 building costs in §4.7.5 (which currently list koku+materials only, though §2.17 BuildableStructure already reserves a coin slot); and (c) the MarketState numbers — base price index per good, saturation increment per sale, recovery rate per in-game day, and how per-shop 'patron/regular' standing (§1.5.2 L278) softens the price-crash. Trade-off: leaving coin numberless keeps §4 shorter but makes coin a dead-end currency and the T1 market unimplementable; conversely, fully specifying it now commits balance before T1 playtesting. Recommended interim: mark coin/market as a known v1-balance hole (deferred to M4 authoring) rather than a frozen spec, so the two "numbers → §4" cross-refs aren't dangling. What is coin actually FOR mechanically in v1, and what is its koku exchange rate?

confirmedFindings.title: The T0 held-koku ledger does not close: ~16K of koku sinks are asserted but never priced, so affordability/soft-lock can't be verified (and 'net' throughput is deducted twice)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.0 L2284 and §4.7.1 L2858 both assert T0 = "~21K produced (~3–5K held)" / "lifetime-produced koku over T0 ≈ 21K (held ≈ 3–5K after spend)". Going from 21.5K produced to 3-5K held requires ~16-18K koku of sinks. But the ONLY koku-denominated sinks defined anywhere in §4 are E1 (400 koku) + E2 (2K koku) = 2.4K (§4.7.5 L2903-2904). Food has no koku price (§2.3 FoodItem L942 = restoreSatiety only); crafting costs are material bundles not koku (§4.7.2 L2870); and debt — narratively 'compound interest … dying' — is modeled as an Estate-Influence GAIN, not a koku drain: §4.2.1 L2397 "Debt tranche cleared → solvency step | Estate | 26 | 200 | 2K". So ~14-16K of the claimed T0 koku spend is unaccounted. Separately, §4.7.1 L2855 calls the per-rung throughput "the net koku-equivalent throughput (after stamina, food, and re-investment)" and then L2858 takes the SAME numbers ×minutes as gross 'lifetime-produced' and deducts spend AGAIN to reach 'held' — food/re-investment netted twice. §4.7.5's affordability tie-out checks E1/E2 against LIFETIME-produced (5.1K/13.3K), never against HELD; held is never computed.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The prompt's core question — does the HELD balance cover concurrent costs (food+tools+crafting+building) without a soft-lock — cannot be answered, because the held figure (3-5K) is asserted, not derived, and the sinks that would produce it don't exist as numbers. If those ~16K of sinks are real, E2's 2K-held purchase at R6 could collide with food/tool spend (soft-lock risk the doc waves away as 'tight, by design'); if they're not real, held ≈ produced ≈ 13-21K and the §4.0 '3-5K held' band is simply wrong. Either way the economy's master bookkeeping is internally inconsistent.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one: either (a) enumerate the koku-denominated food/tool/crafting/re-investment sinks so 21.5K→held actually sums and recompute affordability against HELD at R4/R6; or (b) declare gathering yields already net (held ≈ produced), drop the phantom 'after spend' deduction, and restate the §4.0 held band. Then add an explicit soft-lock check: held ≥ E2 cost at R6 while sustaining food/tool upkeep.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
All quoted text verified verbatim in docs/prd.md. The T0 held-koku figure does not close: §4.0 L2284 and §4.7.1 L2858 assert ~21K produced → 3–5K held, which implies ~16–18K of koku sinks, but the ONLY koku-denominated sinks anywhere in §4 are E1 (400) + E2 (2K) = 2.4K (§4.7.5 L2903-04). Food has no koku price (§2.3 L942: FoodItem = restoreSatiety only), crafting costs are material bundles (§4.7.2 L2870), and debt clearance is an Estate-Influence GAIN not a koku drain (§4.2.1 L2397: 26/200/2K ip). So even taking 21K as gross and subtracting the only priced sinks gives held ≈ 18.6K, not 3–5K. Separately there is a real double-count: L2855 labels the per-rung throughput 'net … after stamina, food, and re-investment,' then L2858 multiplies the same numbers by minutes as gross 'lifetime-produced' and deducts 'spend' AGAIN. The affordability tie-out (§4.7.5 L2906-09) only checks E1/E2 against LIFETIME-produced (5.1K/13.3K), never against HELD, so the prompt's core question — whether the held balance covers concurrent food+tool+crafting+building costs without a soft-lock — cannot be answered from the doc. L2272's claim that the band is 'reconciled' is true only for the produced side, adding false precision. This is distinct from any round-1 produced-band fix (which only aligned 21K≈21.5K on the production side); it concerns the held side and the missing sinks. confirmedFindings.title: The DEV play API (§6.10 / §2.20) omits pacing() and reveals() — the exact methods the QA harness and fun-factor measurement are built on
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.10 (window.__qa block, lines 4232-4244) and §2.20 QaApi (line 1639); vs docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md §1 (lines 50-52) and docs/fun-factor.md §3 (lines 146-160)
confirmedFindings.evidence: qa-playtesting.md §1 lists three Observe methods as the harness's primary interface: `state()`, `pacing()` ("pacing/telemetry object — Proxy metrics accumulated this run (§3)") and `reveals()` ("revealed-entry log — What's been unlocked + when (tick/season) — verifies the reveal cadence"), plus Drive helpers `advanceSeason()/toRung(id)/toTier(n)`. fun-factor.md §3 says the fun targets are measured by "the auto-player + `pacing()` proxies in plans/qa-playtesting.md §3". But the PRD's authoritative API contract in §6.10 lists only: `state, dispatch, tick, frames, pause, resume, newGame, save, load, forceState, setSeed, selectors{unlocked,tier,production}` — and §2.20's QaApi shape is `{ state(), drive(intent), tick/step/frames, pause/resume, new/load/save, force(partialState) }`. A grep across the whole PRD finds zero occurrences of `pacing()`, `reveals()`, `toRung`, `toTier`, or `advanceSeason`. The fun-proxy regression suite (a first-class v1 acceptance bar run "on every build") and the reveal-cadence/novelty-drip proxy therefore depend on telemetry methods the PRD's DEV-API never exposes.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Fun and reveal-cadence are first-class, build-gating quality bars (CLAUDE.md; §7 DoD). If the canonical __qa contract has no pacing()/reveals(), the harness as specified cannot be built without silently re-inventing the interface, and the two satellite docs cite methods that don't exist in the spec they claim to serve.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add `pacing()` and `reveals()` to §6.10's __qa interface (and mirror in §2.20's QaApi shape), and add the time-compression convenience helpers; their return shapes are already described in qa-playtesting.md §1/§3.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed against source. PRD §6.10 (lines 4232-4243) and §2.20 (line 1639) list the __qa/QaApi contract without pacing()/reveals() or the time-compression helpers; grep of docs/prd.md returns zero hits for pacing(), reveals(), toRung, toTier, advanceSeason. Severity downgraded to medium: §6.10's block is explicitly illustrative (uses '...' and 'convenience verb wrappers: ...'), and the satellite fully specifies the methods, so the harness is still buildable — this is contract-completeness/alignment, not a hard blocker. confirmedFindings.title: fun-factor's "recognised deed every ~4.5-5 min" cadence is a T0-only figure but is stated as the global reward-cadence proxy — the locked T2 budget delivers ~13 min/deed
confirmedFindings.location: docs/fun-factor.md §3 (line 155), §2.1 (line 68), §6 reward-deserts (line 257); vs docs/prd.md §4.8.3 (line 3021) and §4.8.1 (line 2966)
confirmedFindings.evidence: fun-factor.md states the reward-cadence proxy threshold as "A reward/unlock/number-jump at least every ~X min; a recognised deed every ~4.5–5 min (§4.8.1)" (§3), repeats it as "the texture is 'a recognised act every ~4.5–5 min' (§4.8.1)" (§2.1) and as the reward-desert guardrail "the ~70% deed stream sized to 'a recognised act every ~4.5–5 min' (§4.8.1)" (§6) — all unqualified by tier. But the cited §4.8.1 is explicitly T0-only: "Deeds across T0 = 30 Estate + 30 Arms = ~56–60 total recognised deeds, one act every ~4.5–5 min" (270 min / 58 ≈ 4.7). The locked T2 budget (§4.8.3) is "Estate 42,000 ip (31 deeds) · Arms 21,000 (20) · Office 35,000 (21)" = ~72 deeds over 16 h (960 min) = one recognised deed every ~13.3 min — ~2.7× slower than the stated floor, by design ("~20–35 deeds/required-pillar ✔"). So the fun-proxy as written either false-fails the entire 16 h T2 tier or labels it a reward-desert by its own number.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: T2 is the doc's named mid-game slump danger zone (§7) — the one tier where accurate fun measurement matters most — yet the recognised-deed proxy is mis-calibrated there. A proxy the locked design cannot satisfy will fire false regressions or get ignored, defeating the fun-regression suite.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide whether the recognised-deed cadence proxy should be tier-relative (e.g. T0 ~5 min, T1 ~5 min, T2 ~13 min) or whether the broader 'reward/number-jump every ~X min' clause (koku/XP/loot between deeds) is what guards reward-deserts while deed-jumps legitimately space out at higher tiers.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed: docs/fun-factor.md states the deed-cadence "a recognised deed/act every ~4.5–5 min (§4.8.1)" three times (lines 68, 155, 257) with no tier qualification, as both the §3 reward-cadence proxy and the §6 reward-desert guardrail ("the ~70% deed stream sized to ~4.5–5 min"). The cited §4.8.1 (docs/prd.md L3009-3012) derives that figure strictly from T0 (270 min / ~58 deeds ≈ 4.7 min). The locked T2 budget (§4.8.3, prd.md ~L3026: Estate 31 + Arms 20 + Office 21 = 72 deeds over 960 min) yields ~13.3 min/deed by design — ~2.8x slower than the stated floor. So a literal reading of the deed-cadence proxy false-fails T2 (the doc's own named slump zone, §7), or the §6 "deed stream sized to ~4.5–5 min" claim is simply factually wrong for T2. The math and text both check out. Downgraded from high to medium because the proxy's actual reward-desert floor is the lead clause "a reward/unlock/number-jump at least every ~X min", and line 313 explicitly leaves ~X min as an unspecified tunable; the dense koku/XP/loot stream between deeds plausibly guards no-dead-time while deeds legitimately space out at higher tiers. This is a proxy-spec calibration/mislabeling issue, not a design-breaking contradiction. needs-human because choosing tier-relative cadence vs. the broad-clause framing is a measurement-design judgment with a real trade-off, and the fix lives in fun-factor.md (a satellite doc) not the PRD body.
confirmedFindings.question: The fun-factor reward-cadence proxy cites a "recognised deed every ~4.5–5 min" as the (untiered) deed-stream sizing target and reward-desert guardrail, but that number is T0-only (§4.8.1); the locked T2 budget (§4.8.3) deliberately spaces recognised deeds to ~13 min/deed (~20–35 deeds/required-pillar over 16 h). A literal proxy reading false-fails T2 — the doc's named mid-game slump zone. Which is intended? (a) The ~4.5–5 min deed cadence is a global fun floor — then the locked T2 16 h budget violates it and would need more frequent deeds; or (b) it's a T0 texture that should be re-expressed as tier-relative (e.g. T0 ~5 min, T1 ~7–8 min, T2 ~13 min) with the broad "reward/number-jump every ~X min" clause (koku/XP/loot between deeds) carrying the no-dead-time/reward-desert guard while deed-jumps legitimately space out at higher tiers? If (b), fun-factor.md §3 (L155) and §6 (L257) and the §2.1 line (L68) should be amended to tier-qualify the deed-cadence figure (or drop the specific number from the reward-desert sizing claim).

confirmedFindings.title: ui-design shows a continuous rate readout on the House-Influence roll-up (家威 +12/koku), contradicting the canon no-time-trickle accrual invariant
confirmedFindings.location: docs/ui-design.md §5.3 (line 339); vs docs/prd.md §1.6.2 / §2.16 / §4.9 hard invariant (line 3079-3081)
confirmedFindings.evidence: ui-design §5.3 illustrates the meter rate-display rule on the Influence roll-up: "Always **show the rate next to the total**: `家威 4.20K (+12/koku)`." But the PRD's HARD INVARIANT (§4.9) is "accrual = jumps + seasonal-judged-on-high-water-mark only (no time-trickle, no flat per-action)"; §2.2(d) "It never grants Influence by itself (no time-trickle)"; §6.6 verifier "no Influence path is a passive per-tick trickle or flat per-action increment." House Influence (家威) and its four pillars have no continuous per-tick/per-koku rate — so a "+12/koku" readout on the 家威 bar is incorrect and visually implies the very trickle the canon forbids.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The bible is the renderer's source of truth for the four-pillar panel (the signature surface). Implementing a per-tick Influence rate on the pillars would either be a fabricated number or leak a trickle the verifier is supposed to ban. The 'show the rate' rule belongs on resources (koku/coin), not the event-driven pillars.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Illustrate the rate rule with a resource, and specify that pillar bars show distance-to-next-gate / last-jump rather than a rate.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed: docs/ui-design.md §5.3 (line 338) illustrates the meter rate rule with `家威 4.20K (+12/koku)`, and §5.7 (line 387) repeats the generic "+12/koku" rate rule. This attaches a continuous/per-unit rate readout to the House-Influence (家威) roll-up on the signature four-pillar panel. That genuinely conflicts with the PRD canon (docs/prd.md lines 3078-3081 HARD INVARIANTS: "accrual = jumps + seasonal-judged-on-high-water-mark only (no time-trickle, no flat per-action)"; §2.4 line 957 and §2.16 line 1428: Influence is "NOT koku and NOT coin... the cumulative score," not a spendable/flowing resource). Influence has no per-tick/per-koku rate, so a "+12/koku" demo on the 家威 bar is a wrong example. BUT two caveats lower this: (1) the PRD under audit is correct as-is — the defect is entirely in the satellite doc ui-design.md, so no prd.md edit applies (this is why I cannot give a prd.md verified_old); (2) it is an illustrative format example, not a behavioral spec — the accrual model is fully and correctly specified elsewhere, so an implementer is unlikely to actually compute a per-tick Influence rate; at worst they'd show a last-jump delta. Hence severity low rather than the claimed medium. Marked needs-human because the fix lives in ui-design.md (not prd.md, against which this was filed) and the replacement requires a UX judgment on what the pillar bars show instead (distance-to-next-gate vs last-deed-jump), which is a design call, not a clerical swap.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">The UI bible (docs/ui-design.md §5.3 line 338, echoed §5.7 line 387) demonstrates the "always show the rate next to the total" meter rule using the House-Influence roll-up: `家威 4.20K (+12/koku)`. But PRD canon forbids any time-trickle / per-action rate on Influence (家威) and its four pillars (accrual = deed-jumps + seasonal only). Question: (a) Re-illustrate the rate rule with a resource that actually flows (e.g. `koku 4.20K (+12/koku)` or coin), AND (b) decide what the event-driven pillar/家威 bars display in lieu of a rate — distance-to-next-gate, the last deed-jump delta, or both. This edit belongs in docs/ui-design.md (the PRD is already correct); no prd.md change is needed.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.old_string: Always **show the rate next to the total**: `家威 4.20K (+12/koku)`.
confirmedFindings.new_string: Always **show the rate next to the total** *for resources*: `koku 4.20K (+12/koku)`. The four-pillar bars instead show **distance-to-next-gate** (and the last deed-jump) — Influence accrues only by event-driven deed-jumps + seasonal judged results, **never** a per-tick rate (PRD §1.6.2 / §2.16 / §4.9 no-time-trickle invariant).

confirmedFindings.title: fun-factor's ≥30-min-floor proxy says "a rung" not "a grind rung" — it would false-fail the blessed ~5-min R0 cold open, diverging from qa-playtesting which correctly scopes it
confirmedFindings.location: docs/fun-factor.md §3 (line 158); vs docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md §3 (line 124) and docs/prd.md §4.8.1 R0 carve-out (lines 2972-2977)
confirmedFindings.evidence: fun-factor.md §3 states the proxy as "Optimal bot can't clear a rung < ~28 min (§4.8); casual bot never *stuck*" — unqualified, so it covers R0. But the PRD blesses an R0 exemption: "R0 floor carve-out — blessed by the human (2026-06-25). The ≥30-min per-rank floor ... applies to the 7 grind rungs R1–R7; R0 is the exempt ~5-min cold-open story rung" (§4.8.1), and §4.8.4 enforces the CI floor only on rungs that should take ≥28 min. qa-playtesting.md §3 already gets this right: "the optimal bot can't clear a **grind rung** in < ~28 min." So the fun-factor proxy, as written, would mark the ~5-min R0 rung a pacing failure and disagrees with its own measurement doc.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A proxy that flags the deliberately-short cold open as a regression undermines trust in the fun-regression suite and contradicts both qa-playtesting.md and the PRD's blessed carve-out. (Round 1 flagged the same R0 gap in PRD §7.1.2; this is a distinct, uncovered instance in the satellite fun doc.)
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Qualify the fun-factor proxy to 'grind rung (R1–R7)' to match qa-playtesting.md §3 and §4.8.1's R0 exemption.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed cross-doc inconsistency. docs/fun-factor.md §3 line 158 states the regression proxy as "Optimal bot can't clear a rung < ~28 min (§4.8)" with no rung qualifier, so a literal reading covers R0. But docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md §3 line 124 already scopes it correctly ("the optimal bot can't clear a grind rung in < ~28 min"), and docs/prd.md §4.8.1 blesses the R0 carve-out (the ≥30-min floor applies only to grind rungs R1–R7; R0 is the exempt ~5-min cold-open story rung). So the fun-factor proxy, read literally, would false-fail the deliberately-short R0 and diverges from both its own measurement doc and the PRD. The PRD is correct; the divergent text is in the satellite fun doc (note: the fix is in docs/fun-factor.md, not docs/prd.md). Distinct from the round-1 R0 gap in PRD §7.1.2. Severity downgraded to low: it is a single missing qualifier in a shorthand proxy-table summary (the operational spec in qa-playtesting is already right), and the same loose "per-rung" shorthand also appears at line 264 — the fix is purely clerical alignment with blessed wording, so trivial-apply.</verdict_reason>
