confirmedFindings.title: ui-design §6.3 pillar-jump colour-flash (--shu gain / --ai loss) contradicts the §2 delta tokens (--ai gain / --beni loss) and spends the reserved seal colour on routine ticks
confirmedFindings.location: docs/ui-design.md §6.3 (line 439); vs §2 palette tokens (lines 75-76, 93, 96) and the §6 motion rule (line 416)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2 fixes the delta semantics as the source of truth: "--delta-pos: var(--ai); /* gains = indigo */", "--delta-neg: var(--beni); /* losses = beni */", and "Deltas use indigo/beni... Gains in --ai, losses in --beni." §2 also reserves vermilion: "The vermilion (--shu) is a stamp, not a coat of paint. It appears on the House seal/crest, the single primary CTA..., and the rank-up beat." But §6.3 (the pillar number-jump, the ~70% routine deed stream) specifies "colour flash toward --shu on gain / --ai on loss." This assigns gain=--shu (the reserved seal colour) and loss=--ai (which §2 designates as the *gain*/positive colour) — flatly inverting/colliding with §2, and violating §6's own rule "reserve big juice for milestones; routine ticks stay quiet."
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The four-pillar panel is the signature surface; a routine deed-jump flashing the rare vermilion seal (and a loss flashing the gain-indigo) breaks the anti-slop seal-reservation discipline and makes gain vs loss visually ambiguous — exactly the 'colour misuse' the bible exists to prevent.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Reconcile §6.3 to the §2 delta tokens (gain pop in --delta-pos/--ai, loss in --delta-neg/--beni) and keep --shu for the rank-up/seal beat only — or, if a vermilion juice-pop on gains is intentionally wanted, amend §2's seal-reservation rule to permit it.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Quotes verified verbatim in docs/ui-design.md: §2 fixes delta semantics as gains=--ai / losses=--beni (lines 75-76, 96) and reserves --shu as a stamp for seal/CTA/rank-up only (lines 93-95); §6 motion rule (line 416) says reserve big juice for milestones, routine ticks stay quiet. §6.3 (line 439) specifies the pillar number-jump pops with a "colour flash toward --shu on gain / --ai on loss." On the signature four-pillar panel — the high-frequency deed stream — this (a) flashes the reserved vermilion seal on routine gains, against §2's seal-reservation and §6's restraint rule, and (b) uses --ai (the §2 gain/positive structural colour) for losses, making gain-vs-loss colour ambiguous on the same numbers where the delta tokens apply. Partial mitigation considered and rejected as fully exonerating: the motion section is self-consistent (§6.2/§6.3/§6.5 all use vermilion=positive, indigo=negative), so this is a deliberate emotional-motion palette rather than a typo — but that very convention collides with the §2 data-delta palette on the same surface, which is exactly the cross-doc inconsistency claimed. Resolving requires a taste/UX judgement (whether a vermilion juice-pop on routine gains is wanted vs. keeping --shu exclusive to milestones), so needs-human, not trivial-apply. Finding is specific to the §6.3-vs-§2 colour collision and reads as new.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">ui-design §6.3 (pillar number-jump) flashes "toward --shu on gain / --ai on loss," but §2 fixes delta colours as gains=--ai (indigo) / losses=--beni and reserves --shu (vermilion) as a stamp for the seal/CTA/rank-up only, while §6 says routine ticks stay quiet. The four-pillar panel is the signature, high-frequency surface. Which intent is canon? Option A: align §6.3 to the §2 delta tokens — gain pop in --delta-pos/--ai, loss in --delta-neg/--beni — keeping vermilion exclusive to rank-up/seal beats (preserves anti-slop seal discipline, but makes the gain pop indigo, the same hue as structural accents, and forfeits a warm 'reward' flash). Option B: keep the §6.3 vermilion gain-pop as an intentional motion convention (matching §6.5 combat win=--shu) and amend §2's seal-reservation rule to permit a transient vermilion juice-flash on gains (more rewarding, but dilutes vermilion's 'stamp not paint' meaning across ~70% of deeds and leaves loss=--ai colliding with --ai as the positive colour). Note that whichever is chosen, the loss colour (currently --ai) should be reconciled so a loss is not flashed in the §2 gain/positive hue.
confirmedFindings.question: Should a routine pillar deed-jump pop in the reserved vermilion (--shu) for extra juice (requiring §2's seal-reservation rule to be loosened), or should §6.3 be aligned to the §2 delta tokens (gain=--ai, loss=--beni) so vermilion stays exclusive to rank-up/seal beats?

confirmedFindings.title: The fun-doc's prescribed T2 anti-slump package is NOT authored anywhere in the PRD content tables — the named cure for the named danger is unwritten
confirmedFindings.location: docs/fun-factor.md §5d L231-239 & §7 T2 L299-303 vs docs/prd.md §3.6 G0–G7 L2021-2028, §4.8.3 L3003-3026, §5 T2.* L3435-3647 (grep for seasonal-twist/re-run/reform/cross-pillar combo returns ZERO hits in prd.md)
confirmedFindings.evidence: fun-factor.md §5d lists four CONCRETE counters "all scheduled into T2": (1) "Front-load T2's biggest new verb … at G0, so the longest tier *opens* with its freshest system"; (2) "Seasonal twists rotate the optimal strategy — change what the seasonal judge rewards each cycle"; (3) "Force re-engagement with early loops at new scale — a T2 reform that lets you re-run estate/village deeds for a new resource"; (4) "The cross-pillar combos 'click' here … pillar synergies become combinatorial." §7 repeats: "deploy the full anti-slump package (§5d)." But NONE are authored in the PRD: a grep across prd.md for "seasonal twist / rotate the optimal / re-run / reform / cross-pillar combo / combinatorial / new scale" returns nothing. §4.8.3 treats T2 seasons as flat ("~120 min/season ⇒ ~8 seasons") with no rotation. And the "front-loaded new verb at G0" is undercut: §3.6 G0 reveals "the trade backbone opens minimally (one route, one porter, one verb)" — the SAME trade/broker verb introduced at T1-V3 (silk/sericulture), now at region scale, i.e. exactly the "×10 version of the old thing" §5b forbids.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The project has correctly diagnosed its single biggest fun risk (the 16 h slump) and written down the cure — but the cure exists only in the fun-doc as intent, with zero corresponding mechanics/content in the PRD that engineering would build. Without the seasonal-twist rotation, the re-run-at-new-scale reform, and concrete cross-pillar combos, T2 is the unmitigated slog the doc warns against.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Author the four §5d devices into the PRD as real content: a genuinely new G0 verb (not region-scale trade), a seasonal-reward rotation schedule (§4.2.2/§4.8.3), a named T2 'reform' that re-runs early deeds for a new resource, and ≥1 concrete cross-pillar combo. Mirror them into §7 milestones so they are built, not just aspired to.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Partially substantiated, but the finding overstates. Grep confirms prd.md has ZERO hits for seasonal-twist/rotate/cross-pillar combo/combinatorial/reform/slump. Of the fun-doc's four §5d anti-slump devices, two are genuinely unauthored in the PRD: (2) seasonal-twist rotation — §4.2.2/§4.8.3 are a flat high-water appraisal with all four pillars judged identically every season, no rotation of what's rewarded; and (4) combinatorial cross-pillar combos — not only absent but in direct tension with §4.3 L2614 ('no cross-pillar leakage except the explicit roll-ups'). However the finding's central claim ('NONE are authored... the cure exists only in the fun-doc with zero corresponding mechanics') is verifiably false for the other two devices: (1) the front-loaded new verb IS authored — G0 (L2021/L3011) opens the trade backbone at the very start of T2 (the '×10 of silk' critique is weak; T1-V3's silk-production sub-engine vs T2's porter/route/sekisho logistics are distinguishable verbs); and (3) the re-run-at-new-scale reform is substantively present — G3 'Asagiri fractally replicated, slimmer' (Hibara+Tōge-mura) and G4 Kuzuhara resettlement re-run village/estate loops at region scale. The bulk of the anti-slump cure (fresh G0→G7 ladder with per-rung new reveals, front-loaded trade verb, fractal re-run, narrative beats spread G2/G4/G6, Edo silhouette) IS authored, so this is a partial gap on two enhancement devices, not an unmitigated slog. Hence medium, not high. needs-human because resolving it is a design decision (author new seasonal-rotation + combo mechanics vs soften the fun-doc's §5d/§7 'full package' language), with a real §4.3 contradiction to adjudicate.
confirmedFindings.question: The fun-doc (§5d/§7) promises a four-part T2 anti-slump 'package,' but the PRD authors only two parts well (front-loaded trade verb at G0; fractal re-run of village loops at region scale via G3 Hibara/Tōge-mura + G4 Kuzuhara). The other two are unbuilt: (a) NO 'seasonal-twist that rotates the optimal strategy' — §4.2.2/§4.8.3 judge all four pillars identically every season with no rotating reward; (b) NO 'combinatorial cross-pillar combos' — and §4.3 (L2614) explicitly forbids cross-pillar leakage 'except the explicit roll-ups,' contradicting the fun-doc. Which path do you want: (1) AUTHOR the two missing devices into the PRD as v1 content — i.e. a concrete seasonal-reward-rotation rule (what does the rotating seasonal judge reward, on what cycle?) in §4.2.2/§4.8.3, plus a defined set of cross-pillar combos that reconciles with or carves an exception to §4.3's no-leakage invariant; or (2) SOFTEN the fun-doc's §5d/§7 'deploy the full anti-slump package' language to match what's actually built (fresh per-rung ladder + front-loaded verb + fractal re-run at scale + spread narrative beats + Edo silhouette), dropping the seasonal-rotation and combinatorial-combo promises; or (3) defer both to T3+ and note them as parked? Note the §4.3 contradiction must be resolved either way.

confirmedFindings.title: Quest-type budget is self-contradictory: locked at "~4 v1 quest types" yet T2 authors BOUNTY, DUEL, and INVESTIGATE as v1 content
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.12(a): "**~4 v1 quest types** … PEST CONTROL, HUNT, CLEAR, DEFEND. Escort/patrol/bounty/duel/expedition/siege/relief/investigate are **parked for T2+**." D-012 codifies the same lean cut-set: "~5 mobs, ~4 quest types, ~6–8 nodes." But v1 = T0–T2, so "parked for T2+" reintroduces them INSIDE v1 — §2.12(e): "Parked quest types reintroduce at T2+." And T2 indeed authors them: Hanzaki is "The recurring DUEL nemesis" (L3618); "The poacher bounty … (BOUNTY archetype)" (L3638); multiple "INVESTIGATE-then-confront one-shots" (§2.13, T2.7). M7 explicitly builds them as v1 work: "Hanzaki is a survived-not-won DUEL nemesis … the 'one-eyed mountain god' … are INVESTIGATE-then-confront one-shots" (L4658). So v1 ships at least ~7 quest archetypes, not 4.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Two failure modes at once. (a) If the headline really is 4 repeatable types across 28.5 h, that is thin variety for the dominant minute-to-minute behaviour. (b) The doc actually needs ~7 to make T2 non-repetitive but the locked cut-set (D-012, §1.11) still advertises 4 — so either the lean-scope ADR undercounts what must be built, or T2 is silently authoring out-of-scope quest types. Engineering scoping off "4 types" would under-build.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Reconcile to one number. Either (a) raise the locked cut-set to the ~7 types v1 actually uses (4 repeatable + bounty/duel/investigate one-shots) and update §2.12/§1.11/D-012, or (b) re-scope T2 to genuinely avoid the parked types. Clarify that "parked for T2+" means post-v1, not T2.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against docs/prd.md. §7.1.1's definitive v1-scope table (L4382) states "Quest types | the 4 types: PEST-CONTROL · HUNT · CLEAR · DEFEND" (no tilde, sourced "canon §I"), and §7.1.1 defines v1 = T0–T2. §2.12(a) L1251-52 and §1.11 L623-24 echo "~4 v1 quest types … Escort/patrol/bounty/duel/…/investigate are parked for T2+", and §2.12(e) L1276 says "Parked quest types reintroduce at T2+." Because T2 is inside v1, "parked for T2+" reintroduces them within v1 — and T2 content does author them: Hanzaki "The recurring DUEL nemesis" (L3618), "The poacher bounty … (BOUNTY archetype)" (L3638), and INVESTIGATE-then-confront one-shots (§2.13 L1295, T2.7 L3634, M5 build phase 3 L4658). So v1 ships ~7 quest archetypes while the locked cut-set advertises 4. This is a genuine, citable internal inconsistency. I down-weight severity to LOW from the claimed medium: the doc has strong mitigating context the finding underweights — §2.12(c)'s data shape lists `…parked…` inside the quest `type` enum, and the prose consistently distinguishes the 4 repeatable grind/standing-earner types from the parked types reintroduced as bespoke one-shots/set-pieces (Hanzaki is a "survived-not-won" nemesis arc, the BOUNTY/INVESTIGATE beats are lean side-quests/belief→cause one-shots per §2.12(b)/§2.13). M5 phase 3 already plans to author them, so under-build risk is modest. The defect is really a wording/scoping-clarity contradiction ("parked for T2+" vs v1=T0–T2 vs "the 4 types"), not a missing-build. Fix requires a design/scope judgment (clarify the budget as 4 repeatable + 3 one-shots, or re-word "T2+"→post-v1), so needs-human, not trivial-apply. The finding (one quest-type budget number vs the count v1 uses) appears distinct from a mob-count or other round-1 concern, so treated as new.
confirmedFindings.question: The v1 scope is stated two ways that conflict on quest-type count: the definitive cut-set table (§7.1.1 L4382), §2.12(a)/§1.11, and D-012 all say v1 has "the 4 quest types" (PEST-CONTROL/HUNT/CLEAR/DEFEND), with the others "parked for T2+"; but v1 = T0–T2, and T2 explicitly authors DUEL (Hanzaki, L3618), BOUNTY (poacher, L3638), and INVESTIGATE one-shots (§2.13/T2.7/M5 phase 3). Which is canonical for the v1 budget: (a) keep "4" as the *repeatable grind* quest types and add a one-line note that bounty/duel/investigate reappear at T2 only as bespoke *one-shots* outside the lean repeatable cut-set (and reword "parked for T2+" → "parked for T3+/post-v1" to remove the literal contradiction); or (b) raise the locked cut-set in §7.1.1/§2.12/§1.11/D-012 to the ~7 archetypes v1 actually uses (4 repeatable + duel/bounty/investigate one-shots)? Option (a) preserves the lean-scope headline and is purely clarifying; option (b) makes the scope ADR match the authored content but inflates the advertised budget. Either way the phrase "parked for T2+" should not collide with v1 = T0–T2.

confirmedFindings.title: The 2-3 weapon lines are named LOCKED v1 content but never enumerated or mechanically differentiated; the combat math treats every weapon skill identically
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md 7.1.1 line 4380 + 2.7(e) line 1088 (LOCKED '2-3 weapon lines') vs 4.6.1 lines 2758/2761 (generic weaponSkill terms)
confirmedFindings.evidence: Line 4380 lists the LOCKED v1 skill set including '2-3 weapon lines' and line 1088 repeats it, but nowhere does the PRD say WHAT the lines are or how they differ. The only weapon-line hooks in the combat math are generic: attackPower's skillBonus = 0.3*weaponSkillLevel (line 2758) and accuracy = 10 + 0.4*AGI + 0.3*weaponSkillLevel (line 2761). There is no per-line speed/reach/damage/targetCount archetype (no fast-low-damage vs slow-high-damage, no spear-reach/multi-target vs sword). EquipDef.statMods differentiates individual weapons, but the SKILL LINE you invest in is mechanically a pure reskin. M2a phase 1 (line 4529) requires 'combat/weapon skills are registered' with no spec to build from. Grep for reach/two-hand/polearm/fast-weapon/heavy-weapon returns nothing mechanical.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Weapon-line choice is supposed to be a core replayability axis ('is one obviously best?'). As specced it is either (a) undefined - blocking the M2a build, since there is no data telling the engine how spear differs from sword; or (b) defined as identical functions of weaponSkillLevel - i.e. flat sameness, the boredom risk the lens names. Either way there is no balanced trade-off between lines and no reason to replay with a different weapon.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Enumerate the 2-3 v1 weapon lines (e.g. yari/spear = reach + targetCount>1 + slower; uchigatana/sword = balanced; kodachi or staff = fast/low-damage) and give each a differentiating stat profile (base speed, base damage, targetCount, crit affinity) plus its own L10/L50 milestone identity, so each line implies a different attribute pairing and play pattern.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Factual claims verified: §7.1.1 (line 4380) and §2.7(e) (line 1088) lock "2-3 weapon lines" as v1 content, but the PRD never enumerates which lines ship, and the §4.6.1 combat math (lines 2758/2761) feeds every weapon skill through a single generic `weaponSkillLevel` with no per-line term. Per-line archetype properties are genuinely absent from the data model: `targetCount` is a `Stance` field (line 1122) and `EquipDef.statMods` (line 1191) is attack/defense per individual item — there is no reach/multi-target/archetype property attached to a weapon line. So weapon-line *identity* (the stated replayability axis) is underspecified. However, the finding's framing overstates severity on two points: (1) M2a is explicitly scoped to only the crude carrying-pole + wolf fixture (line 4533) with weapon-line content deferred to M2b (line 4546) and balanced at M6, so the "blocks M2a" claim is false — M2a needs at most one registered combat skill, not the full enumeration; (2) differentiation hooks DO exist — individual weapons differ via statMods/weaponBase and per-skill milestones (§4.5.3, "L50: stance slot / signature ability") can carry distinct line identities. The real, narrower gap is that the PRD names a build-diversity axis without ever specifying the lines or what makes choosing one a differentiated decision. That is a design/content decision with genuine trade-offs (which lines, what archetype, which attribute pairing), hence needs-human, and medium not high since the filling mechanisms already exist and the content is deferred, not blocking.
confirmedFindings.question: Which 2-3 weapon lines ship in v1, and what gives each a distinct mechanical identity so weapon choice is a real replayability axis rather than a stat reskin? Specifically: (a) name the lines (e.g. yari/spear, uchigatana/sword, kodachi or staff); (b) decide whether per-line archetype lives in equipment data (extend EquipDef.statMods with reach/targetCount/base-speed so a spear inherently multi-targets or out-reaches a sword) versus the skill line itself versus per-line milestone perks (the §4.5.3 L50 signature ability) — currently targetCount is a Stance-only field and statMods is attack/defense only, so no weapon archetype is expressible; (c) state each line's attribute pairing (e.g. spear→AGI/reach, sword→balanced STR/AGI, staff→SPD/fast-low-damage) and its L10/L50 milestone identity. Trade-off: full archetype differentiation (reach, multi-target, speed/damage curves) maximizes build diversity but adds combat-math and balance surface (the M6 win-rate band must hold per line); leaving lines as statMods-only proficiency tracks is cheaper and already buildable but risks the "is one obviously best / flat sameness" boredom the lens names. This is non-blocking for M2a (crude-pole-only) but should be locked before M2b authors the weapon-line content.

confirmedFindings.title: Stances - a core build/intervention axis - have no defined roster, and the Stance type vs the combat formulas disagree on which fields exist
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md 2.8(c) line 1122 (Stance type) vs 4.6.1 lines 2758-2762 (formula field usage); M2a phase 1 line 4529 ('stances are defined')
confirmedFindings.evidence: Stance is defined only as a TYPE: Stance { attackMod, speedMod, evasionMod, targetCount } (line 1122). No concrete stances (e.g. aggressive/defensive/guard) with values are ever specified anywhere in 4.6 or 4.x, yet M2a phase 1 (line 4529) asserts 'stances are defined.' Worse, the type and the formulas are mutually inconsistent: defense = gearDef + 0.5*STR + stance.defMod (line 2762) reads a defMod field that the Stance type does NOT declare, while attackPower (line 2758) does NOT read stance.attackMod even though the type declares it; and targetCount is declared but no 4.6 damage formula handles multi-target. So one stance lever is unreachable, one is dangling, and the whole roster is unspecced.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Stances are the primary in-fight strategic choice ('low-APM, strategic') and a build/replay axis. With no concrete stance roster and contradictory fields, the stance system is hollow: you cannot build it (no values), the defense lever the formula needs is missing from the type, and the attack lever the type advertises does nothing. This undercuts the only moment-to-moment build expression combat offers.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Author a small v1 stance roster with values (e.g. Aggressive: +attack -evasion; Defensive: +def/+evasion -attack-rate; Even). Reconcile the type and formulas: either wire stance.attackMod into attackPower or drop it; add defMod to the type (see build-diversity-6); and specify how targetCount affects resolution (or defer multi-target with a note).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed in source. The Stance type (line 1122) declares {attackMod, speedMod, evasionMod, targetCount}, but the §4.6.1 formulas (lines 2758-2762) use stance.speedMod and stance.evasionMod (declared), read stance.defMod in the defense formula (NOT declared in the type), and never read stance.attackMod in attackPower (declared but dangling). targetCount is declared but no §4.6 damage formula handles multi-target (though §5/line 3239 flags multi-target as an intended mechanic). Grep confirms no concrete stance roster exists anywhere — only the type, a StanceId/set_stance intent (3991), an L50 'stance slot' unlock, and the M2a phase-1 assertion 'stances are defined' (4529) which has no data to build against. Stances are the primary in-fight strategic/build lever, so a hollow roster plus contradictory fields is a real gap. Distinct from sibling build-diversity-6 (which isolates the defMod field); not a round-1 restatement since the contradictory text survives round-1. Needs-human: resolving requires design choices (roster names/values, wiring vs dropping attackMod, multi-target now vs deferred).</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Stances are the core in-fight build/strategy lever but are unspecced and the type/formulas disagree. Decide: (1) the v1 stance roster — concrete names with attackMod/defMod(or attackMod?)/speedMod/evasionMod/targetCount values (e.g. Aggressive +attack -evasion, Defensive +def/+evasion -rate, Even baseline); (2) reconcile fields — the defense formula (line 2762) reads stance.defMod which the Stance type (line 1122) does not declare, and attackPower (line 2758) ignores stance.attackMod which the type does declare: either add defMod to the type and wire attackMod into attackPower, or rename/drop fields so type and formulas match exactly; (3) targetCount — does v1 implement multi-target resolution (and via what damage rule), or is it deferred with an explicit note? M2a phase 1 (line 4529) asserts 'stances are defined' and the combat engine cannot be built without this concrete data and a consistent field set.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: What is the v1 stance roster (names + attackMod/defMod/speedMod/evasionMod/targetCount values), should attackPower apply stance.attackMod, and does v1 implement multi-target via targetCount or defer it? The combat engine (M2a) needs concrete stance data and a consistent field set to build.

confirmedFindings.title: Stance type omits the defMod field that the defense formula reads (undefined field reference)
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md 2.8(c) line 1122 (Stance type) vs 4.6.1 line 2762 (defense formula)
confirmedFindings.evidence: Line 2762: defense = gearDef + 0.5*STR + stance.defMod. Line 1122 declares Stance { attackMod, speedMod, evasionMod, targetCount } - no defMod. The defense formula references a field the type does not declare. Grep confirms line 1122 is the only Stance type shape and 'defMod' appears only in the formula at line 2762.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A combat formula reads a struct field that does not exist on the type; the M2a engine cannot compile the defense path as written. This is a clerical type/formula mismatch independent of the broader stance-design question in build-diversity-4.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add defMod to the Stance type so the defense formula's reference resolves.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified: line 2762 defense formula reads `stance.defMod`, but the Stance DATA-shape at line 1122 declares only `{ attackMod, speedMod, evasionMod, targetCount }`. Grep confirms these are the only Stance shape and the only defMod reference. The formula already commits to the field, so declaring it on the type is a pure clerical alignment with no design/balance trade-off. Distinct from any broader stance-design concern (this is solely the undeclared field).

confirmedFindings.title: The combat difficulty curve is calibrated at exactly ONE point (R3 wolf); every later mob/rung win-rate is unspecified and the pacing/fun regression only measures economy + the first fight
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §4.8.1–§4.8.3 (lines 2952-3026), §4.6.6 (lines 2806-2814), §7.2 M5 line 4692, M2a lines 4520-4522; docs/fun-factor.md line 160
confirmedFindings.evidence: The ONLY combat-difficulty anchor in the whole spec is the R3 wolf: §4.6.6 'wins only ~20–35% of the time' rising to '~85%+'. fun-factor.md line 160's combat proxy is identical and first-fight-only: 'Combat tension band | First-fight win ~20–35%; climbs to ~85% over the rung (§4.6.6)'. The §4.8.1-3 pacing tables track only 'Gating cost' (koku/deeds/ip) and 'wall-clock' — never a win-rate. The headless regression tests are economic ('Optimal bot can't clear a rung < ~28 min'; M5 line 4692 prints only 'per-rung tick→wall-clock'), and M2a's only combat-difficulty assertion is 'the fresh-MC first-fight win-rate sits in 20–35% and a post-drills MC ~85%+' (lines 4521-4522). Yet later mobs are flagged as power spikes with NO win-rate target — e.g. T1.6 wolves pack 'First fight that punishes a lone, under-geared player' (line 3394) and G1's 'region-scale combat (ronin/bandits)'. So the 'smooth ramp' is asserted but unmeasured past R3.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Combat is a first-class v1 pillar, but its difficulty curve is a single calibrated point with ~12 uncalibrated mobs and ~8 combat rungs after it. A spike (wolf pack at V2, ronin at G1, Hanzaki at G5) or a trough cannot be caught by any regression test or fun-proxy, so a power-vs-challenge cliff would ship silently — exactly the 'wall' the design forbids.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a per-rung (or per-mob) expected win-rate band to the §4.8 pacing tables and a second fun-proxy row that asserts a target win-rate curve at the key combat rungs (R3, V2, V5, G1, G5), then unit/headless-test it the way M2a tests the R3 wolf.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Facts verified: the only calibrated/LOCKED combat-difficulty anchor in the spec is the R3 wolf (§4.6.6 lines 2806-2814: 20-35% rising to 85%+). fun-factor.md line 160's "Combat tension band" proxy is explicitly first-fight-only and cites §4.6.6. The §4.8.1-3 pacing tables (lines 2952-3026) track gating cost + wall-clock and surface combat only as gate conditions, never a per-rung win-rate. M2a's only win-rate assertion tests the R3 wolf (lines 4521-4522), and §4.9 (lines 3064-3066) confirms combat mob magnitudes are LEVERS with only the R3 first-fight win-rate LOCKED. So no automated regression test or fun-proxy measures combat difficulty across the ~8 combat rungs / ~12 mobs after R3 — a genuine coverage hole that conflicts with §4.8's stated "retune that breaks pacing fails CI" philosophy. However, severity is overstated as high: (1) the §4.6 combat math is fully deterministic/formulaic, so per-rung win-rate emerges from the defined §4.0 number spine (MC stats vs bestiary stats), not a free parameter; (2) combat magnitudes are deliberately levers tuned in the M6 balance pass, so there is intentionally no locked target to regress against; (3) the pacing tables do encode deliberate combat design points (e.g. G5 Hanzaki "survived, not won"). This is a real testability/measurement gap requiring a human design decision, hence medium / needs-human, not a clerical fix.
confirmedFindings.question: Combat difficulty is calibrated and CI-tested at exactly one point (the R3 first fight: 20-35% -> 85%, LOCKED), but the ~8 later combat rungs and ~12 mobs have no expected win-rate target, and neither the §4.8 pacing tables nor the fun-factor "Combat tension band" proxy (first-fight-only) measure combat difficulty past R3 — even though combat is a first-class v1 pillar and §4.8's whole philosophy is regression-tested pacing. Do you want to (A) commit an explicit win-rate-vs-rung band (the combat analogue of the koku/ip pacing table) at the key combat rungs (R3, V2, V5, G1, G5) and headless-test it the way M2a tests the R3 wolf, accepting that this locks more of the combat curve and constrains the M6 balance pass; or (B) deliberately leave combat difficulty to hand-tuning/taste with only the R3 anchor locked, relying on the deterministic §4.6 math + M6 balance pass to keep the ramp smooth and accepting that a combat spike/trough would not be caught by any automated test? Trade-off: (A) closes the testability gap but over-constrains a system the spec currently treats as levers; (B) keeps flexibility but ships combat difficulty with no automated guardrail.

confirmedFindings.title: The 25%→85% recovery is mislocated: §4.6.6 says it happens 'over real R3 play', but 85% needs weaponSkill 5 + a crafted blade, while R3 reaches only weaponSkill 3 and crafting opens at R4
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.6.6: 'After ~a season of drills (weaponSkill ~5, attrs ~10, a smith-forged blade), win-rate against that same wolf is ~85%+ ... "~a season of drills" is now ~30–40 min of real R3 play (the per-rank floor) — the climb from ~25 % to ~85 % is felt as earned over real time'. But R3's own gating budget (§4.8.1 line 2957) only reaches 'weaponSkill→~3', not 5; the crafted/smith-forged blade requires the 'simple Crafting tab + loot→craft loop (Smith Gonta)' which §3.2 R4 (line 1802) and §2.10 (line 1181, 'Smith Gonta's forge → ... blades') gate to R4, not R3; and weaponSkill 5 milestones land around R5. So the stated 85% endpoint cannot be reached within R3.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The headline emotional payoff of the difficulty curve — feeling the 25%→85% climb 'over real R3 play' — is arithmetically impossible with R3's stated progression (weaponSkill 3, no crafting). The recovery actually spans R3→R5 (~105 min), so either the §4.6.6 self-correction note is wrong or R3's budget is, and the locked first-fight curve is mis-described.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Correct §4.6.6 to locate the 85% recovery where the prerequisites actually exist (R4 crafting + ~R5 weaponSkill 5), e.g. 'felt over R3–R5', or raise R3's weaponSkill/gear budget so the recovery genuinely completes inside R3 — but reconcile the two.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed at source. §4.6.6 (prd.md lines 2810-2814) sets the 85%+ wolf win-rate endpoint behind "weaponSkill ~5, attrs ~10, a smith-forged blade," then its parenthetical note equates "~a season of drills" with "~30–40 min of real R3 play (the per-rank floor)" and says the 25%→85% climb is "felt as earned over real time." But §4.8.1's pacing table contradicts both prerequisites for R3: R3 (line 2957) reaches only "weaponSkill→~3," and crafting (the "simple Crafting + loot→craft loop," Smith Gonta) does not open until R4 (line 2958), so no smith-forged blade can exist in R3. Therefore the 85% endpoint's own stated prerequisites cannot be met within R3 — they land at R4 (crafting) and ~R5 (weaponSkill 5). The §4.6.6 note's "over real R3 play (the per-rank floor)" framing is genuinely inconsistent with the gating. Not a clerical fix: reconciling it means either re-scoping the recovery to R3–R5 in §4.6.6 or raising R3's weaponSkill/gear budget — a balance/design trade-off the human must arbitrate, especially since the 20–35% first-fight target and the curve shape are LOCKED canon.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">§4.6.6 says the wolf-fight recovery from ~25% to ~85% is "felt as earned over ~30–40 min of real R3 play (the per-rank floor)," but the 85% endpoint it specifies needs weaponSkill ~5 + a smith-forged blade, while §4.8.1 caps R3 at weaponSkill ~3 and opens crafting (Smith Gonta) only at R4 — so 85% is unreachable inside R3 and actually spans R3→~R5 (~105 min). Which side should change: (a) edit §4.6.6 to locate the 85% recovery where its prerequisites exist (e.g. "felt over R3–R5"), keeping R3's budget as-is; or (b) raise R3's weaponSkill/gear budget (and pull crafting earlier) so the recovery genuinely completes inside R3? The locked items (20–35% first-fight win, the curve shape) are unaffected either way; this is about where the climb's endpoint lands.</parameter>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Where should the wolf-win recovery to ~85% actually land — inside R3 (which would require boosting R3's weaponSkill/crafting budget) or across R3–R5 (which means editing §4.6.6's 'over real R3 play' claim) — given crafting only opens at R4 and weaponSkill 5 at ~R5?

confirmedFindings.title: Accessibility/audio controls have no always-available entry point — Settings is absent from the nav-reveal ladder, so textScale/colourblindMode/mute/pause are unreachable at the cold open when an a11y-dependent player needs them first
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §3.5 nav-reveal (lines 1957-1967) & §6.9 (line 4205); docs/ui-design.md §5.6 (line 376); §6.11 (line 4257)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.11 promises a11y basics are 'wired so they cannot be an afterthought' (line 4257), including a `textScale` setting, `colourblindMode`, a `mute` toggle and a `pause`. But the entry points are never made always-available. The §3.5 nav-reveal table (lines 1959-1967) ladders the nav as: '(none) — single column' → Skills → Combat → Map → House → Village → Region → Ties → Castle-town — **with no Settings row at all**. ui-design.md §5.6 (line 376) repeats the same order: 'single column (none) → Skills → Combat → Map → House → Village → Region → Ties → Castle-town stub' — again no Settings. §6.9 (lines 4203-4205) lists 'Settings screens' among the example screens but states 'nav and screens are revealed as earned — so it appears single-screen early (minute one is one verb + the log)'. The cold open (line 1959) is 'Just the event log + verb(s) ... No nav exists yet.' Net: a colourblind, photosensitive, audio-sensitive, or low-vision player who needs colourblindMode / mute / pause / textScale at the very first screen has no specified way to reach them, and the progressive-reveal nav never surfaces Settings.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Accessibility settings are exactly the controls a player must reach BEFORE they can use the game, not after they 'earn' them. Browser zoom partially covers textScale and OS prefers-reduced-motion partially covers reducedMotion, but colourblindMode, the in-game mute, and pause have no OS backstop and would be unreachable until an unspecified late reveal. This directly contradicts the 'cannot be an afterthought' framing and D16.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify an always-available Settings/a11y affordance (e.g. a persistent gear in the global header described in ui-design §4.1) that is exempt from progressive reveal and present from the cold open, and add a Settings/a11y row to the §3.5 and §5.6 reveal narratives marking it as available from minute one.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against source. §3.5's nav-reveal table (prd.md lines 1959-1967) ladders the nav with no Settings row; ui-design.md §5.6 (lines 374-375) repeats the same order ('single column (none) → Skills → Combat → Map → House → Village → Region → Ties → Castle-town stub') with no Settings; and §6.9 (lines 4202-4205) explicitly groups 'Settings screens' among surfaces where 'nav and screens are revealed as earned.' The cold open is specified as verb + log only (prd.md line 1959 'No nav exists yet'; ui-design.md line 529-532 'one seal-less verb button'). The global header is defined as 'vitals: key resources + next-gate' (ui-design.md §4.1 line 198) — no settings/gear affordance. §2.21(e) (line 1674-1675) only claims a11y basics 'exist from the first build', which is about the features existing, not about a reachable entry point. So colourblindMode, in-game mute, and pause (no OS backstop) have no specified way to be reached at the cold open, which sits in tension with §6.11's 'cannot be an afterthought.' I down-rate from high to medium: the cold open is very brief before the first nav, and textScale/reducedMotion have partial OS/browser backstops, so this is a design-completeness gap rather than a shipped-blocker. It is needs-human because placement involves a genuine UX trade-off — a persistent header gear conflicts with the deliberately empty cold-open *ma* (ui-design line 530 'Maximum ma — the empty paper reads as confident'). Appears to be a new accessibility-entry-point gap distinct from balance/gating round-1 fixes. Could not enumerate round-1 findings to fully exclude restatement, but the entry-point framing is specific and not reflected in the applied-fix text.
confirmedFindings.question: Where should the accessibility/audio/comfort controls (colourblindMode, in-game mute, pause, textScale) live so they are reachable from the very first screen, given the cold open is deliberately bare (one verb + log, 'maximum ma')? Options: (A) a single persistent affordance — a small gear in the global header — that is exempt from progressive reveal and present from the cold open, accepting a minor compromise to the empty-paper first impression; (B) an always-present overflow/drawer entry that is visually quieter than a header gear; or (C) accept the current reveal model and rely on OS/browser backstops, explicitly documenting that in-game colourblindMode/mute/pause are intentionally deferred until the first nav reveal. If (A) or (B): add a 'Settings/a11y — available from minute one (exempt from reveal)' row to the §3.5 table and the ui-design §5.6 nav narrative, and reconcile §6.9's wording that currently lists 'Settings screens' among the revealed-as-earned surfaces.

confirmedFindings.title: Mobile information architecture at T2 is undefined: ~8-9 revealed nav surfaces vs a 3-5-slot bottom tab-bar, with no rule for the primary/overflow split — and in tension with 'same entries, same order, both layouts'
confirmedFindings.location: docs/ui-design.md §8 (lines 485-487) vs §3.5 nav ladder (lines 1959-1967)
confirmedFindings.evidence: ui-design §8 (line 485) states 'Desktop nav rail and mobile bottom-bar/drawer grow the same number of entries in the same order', then (lines 486-487) 'Mobile: bottom tab-bar for the 3–5 primary areas (thumb reach); secondary/overflow in a drawer/sheet'. But by T2 the §3.5 nav ladder reveals Skills, Combat, Map, House, Village, Region, Ties, plus the Castle-town stub — roughly 8-9 surfaces. A bottom bar capped at '3–5 primary areas' cannot hold them, so most live in the overflow drawer — yet the spec never defines WHICH entries are primary vs overflow, how that split is chosen, or how it re-shuffles each time a new surface reveals. 'Same entries in the same order' (desktop rail) is therefore not literally realizable on the bottom bar, and the IA for the multi-tab T2 shell on the smallest viewport is unspecified.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: T2 is the bulk of v1 playtime and the densest UI; mobile is a first-class target (responsive, thumb-reach). Without a defined primary/overflow model the most-used screens may be buried in a drawer, and the reveal beat ('each new entry slides in') is undefined for entries that land in overflow.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Define the mobile primary set (which 3-5 screens occupy the bottom bar at each tier) and the overflow rule, and reconcile the 'same entries in the same order' claim with the bottom-bar cap (e.g. 'primary bar = current-tier core screens; older/secondary screens demote to the drawer in reveal order').
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Cited text is accurate (ui-design.md §8 lines 485-487 and §5.6 lines 372-380; PRD §3.5 nav ladder reveals 8 surfaces by T2). A repo-wide grep confirms the doc never defines WHICH revealed entries are the 3-5 mobile-bottom-bar 'primary' set vs drawer 'overflow', nor how that split re-allocates as each new surface reveals. So the gap is real. However the finding's 'contradiction with same entries, same order' is overstated: the spec explicitly routes overflow to a drawer, and bar+drawer together can preserve reveal order, so there is no literal contradiction. The model (bottom bar + drawer, reveal order) IS specified at guideline level; only the per-tier primary allocation is missing. Given a conventional default pattern exists and the project's bias-to-action ethos lets the agent pick primaries at build/playtest time, this is a low-severity under-specification, not medium. Choosing the primary set is a UX judgment, so needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: For the mobile bottom tab-bar (capped at 3-5 thumb-reach slots), which screens are 'primary' when ~8 are revealed by T2, and what rule governs the primary/overflow split as each new surface reveals? Options: (a) primary bar = the current tier's core/most-used screens, older/secondary screens demote to the drawer in reveal order (e.g. T2 bar = Work, Combat, Map, House, Village; Skills/Region/Ties/Castle-town in drawer); (b) fixed primary set (e.g. Work, Map, House, Village, Combat) with everything else always in drawer; (c) treat 3-5 as a soft guideline and let build/playtest determine allocation. Trade-off: an explicit per-tier rule guarantees the most-used screens stay on the bar and defines the slide-in reveal beat for entries that land in overflow, but adds spec/maintenance burden to a design bible that elsewhere defers implementation detail.

confirmedFindings.title: ARIA live-region scope/politeness is unspecified — the whole 5-channel event log as one live region would flood assistive tech with combat/reward/system noise; and the a11y acceptance only runs keyboard+touch, never a screen-reader pass
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.11 (line 4268) & §7.2 M7 phase 6 (line 4697); docs/ui-design.md §5.1 (lines 312-322)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.11 (line 4268): 'the event/story log is an ARIA live region so reveals/important events are announced to assistive tech'. ui-design §5.1 (line 312) repeats it, but the same log carries FIVE channels (lines 316-322): narration, reward (every `+N`), combat (every fight outcome / `⚔️` line), system (restocks, saves — 'the quietest tier'), and milestone/reveal. Nothing specifies the live-region politeness level (polite vs assertive) or scopes announcements to important channels only. As written, a single live region wrapping the whole log would queue every reward mote, every combat swing, and every restock/save line to a screen reader — overwhelming and exactly the opposite of 'reveals/important events are announced'. Compounding this, the only a11y verification (M7 phase 6, line 4697; cf. M2b line 4686) is 'a keyboard-only and a touch-only run' — neither exercises a screen reader, so the live-region behaviour is asserted but never actually heard/tested.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: An over-broad polite/assertive live region is a classic AT-flooding failure that makes a game unusable with a screen reader, while the spec's intent ('important events') is the opposite. With no channel scoping and no AT test in the acceptance gate, the regression can't be caught.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify the politeness level and scope: e.g. announce only narration + milestone/reveal channels (aria-live=polite), exclude per-tick reward/combat/system from the live region (or batch them), and add a screen-reader pass to the §6.11 acceptance, not just keyboard+touch.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Quoted text verified verbatim: §6.11 line 4268 ("the event/story log is an ARIA live region so reveals/important events are announced to assistive tech"); ui-design §5.1 line 312 repeats it; lines 316-322 confirm the five channels (narration, reward per +N, combat per ⚔️ outcome, system restocks/saves, milestone/reveal). No politeness level (polite/assertive) or channel-to-live-region scoping is specified anywhere in the PRD or ui-design. The only a11y acceptance gates — M2b (line 4686) and M7 phase 6 (line 4697) — verify via a keyboard-only and a touch-only run; neither exercises a screen reader, so the live-region behaviour is asserted but never heard/tested. This is a genuine, distinct accessibility gap (live-region politeness/scope + missing SR pass), not a restatement of the keyboard/touch/colourblind basics. Skeptical downgrade to low: the spec already says "reveals/important events" and the DoD says "live-region announces reveals," so the flooding-of-every-reward-mote scenario is partly a strawman (scoping intent is gestured at), and this is intentionally high-level PRD "accessibility basics" altitude. needs-human because choosing which channels announce and at what politeness is a UX judgement with a real trade-off.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">For the event-log ARIA live region (§6.11 / ui-design §5.1), pin two things the PRD currently leaves open: (1) Politeness + scope — which of the five log channels are announced to assistive tech, and at what aria-live level? Proposed default: announce only `narration` + `milestone/reveal/rank-up` at aria-live=polite, and exclude per-tick `reward`, per-swing `combat`, and `system` (restocks/saves) from the live region (or batch them) so a screen reader is not flooded — matching the spec's stated intent of "reveals/important events." (2) Acceptance — should the a11y gate (M2b line 4686 / M7 phase 6 line 4697) add a screen-reader pass, or is the current keyboard-only + touch-only run sufficient (leaving live-region behaviour asserted but never actually heard)?
confirmedFindings.question: Which log channels should the ARIA live region announce (and at what politeness)? Announcing all five — including per-reward and per-combat-swing lines — would flood a screen reader; the spec currently wraps the whole log without scoping, and the acceptance test is keyboard/touch only.

confirmedFindings.title: Kanji-only labels with aria-hidden emoji give screen-reader users bare CJK glyphs (pillars, rank names, season tags) with no romaji/English accessible name or lang tag; K/M/B numbers are also unexpanded for AT
confirmedFindings.location: docs/ui-design.md §7 (line 455), §5.3 pillar table (lines 344-350), §5.7 (lines 382-389); docs/prd.md §6.11 (line 4269)
confirmedFindings.evidence: ui-design §7 (line 455): 'Emoji are decorative (aria-hidden="true"); the kanji/word always carries the meaning.' The four-pillar panel (§5.3, lines 344-350) renders each pillar as a kanji label (武威 / 官威 / 家産 / 家格 / 家威) + an aria-hidden emoji + a colour; rank cards (§5.4) show kanji titles; season tags are 春/夏/秋/冬. With the emoji hidden and meaning carried 'by the kanji', a non-Japanese screen-reader user is left with a bare CJK glyph and no specified accessible-name alternative (no romaji/English aria-label, no `lang` attribute to route pronunciation). §6.11 (line 4269) only promises 'semantic landmarks and labelled controls' — but the labels themselves are kanji. Separately, §5.7 (lines 382-389) renders values as abbreviated '12.4K / 3.1M' with no expanded accessible value, which AT reads ambiguously. (Round 1's glossary finding was about VISIBLE glossing for sighted non-JP players; this is the distinct assistive-tech path.)
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The pillar panel, ranks, and seasons are core readouts; for a screen-reader user they are the game's primary feedback, and bare unlabelled kanji + hidden emoji + abbreviated numbers means AT conveys little or mispronounces. The 'kanji always carries the meaning' rule is true for sighted players but is an AT dead-end.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Require an accessible name (romaji + English, e.g. aria-label='Arms (武威)') and `lang="ja"` on kanji glyph spans, expose an unabbreviated aria value for K/M/B readouts (e.g. aria-label='12,400'), and verify with a screen-reader pass.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Quoted text verified: ui-design.md §7 (line 455) states "Emoji are decorative (aria-hidden=\"true\"); the kanji/word always carries the meaning"; the §5.3 pillar panel renders each pillar as a kanji label + aria-hidden emoji + colour; §5.7 fixes resource readouts to K/M/B with no expanded value; and prd.md §6.11 commits only to "semantic landmarks and labelled controls" plus the live-region log. None require a romaji/English accessible name, lang="ja", or expanded numeric aria values, so a non-JP screen-reader user tabbing to the pillar/season readouts receives bare CJK glyphs and abbreviated numbers. This is a genuine a11y completeness gap and is distinct from the round-1 visible-glossing finding (that concerned sighted non-JP players; this is the assistive-tech path). Severity reduced from medium to low because the claim is partly overstated: §5.4 rank cards already carry a visible romanized rank (read by AT), and the ARIA live-region event/story log carries the primary narrative feedback in English prose, so AT users are not left with "little" overall — only the secondary pillar/season chrome is affected. Fix is needs-human: it is an a11y policy decision (accessible-name scheme, lang tagging, numeric expansion) that trades against the woodblock/kanji-first aesthetic and v1 scope, not a clerical edit. The PRD is a design doc, so this is a forward-looking specification gap rather than a shipped defect.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Should the a11y spec (prd §6.11 / ui-design §7) require that kanji glyph readouts carry an explicit accessible name (romaji + English, e.g. aria-label="Arms (武威)") plus lang="ja", and that K/M/B values expose an unabbreviated aria value (e.g. aria-label="12,400") — verified by a screen-reader pass? Trade-off: this closes a real screen-reader gap on the core pillar/season readouts (a11y is canon §H), but adds per-label authoring/maintenance overhead and must not visually intrude on the kanji-first woodblock aesthetic; it is partly mitigated already because rank cards carry visible romanization and the event log is an English ARIA live region. Options: (a) require accessible names + lang + expanded numeric aria for all kanji/abbreviated readouts now; (b) require it only for the pillar panel and primary vitals; (c) defer to a later a11y-hardening milestone with an explicit note.
confirmedFindings.question: Should kanji labels and K/M/B readouts carry explicit romaji/English accessible names (and lang tags / expanded numeric aria values) for screen-reader users? As written, meaning rides on the kanji with the emoji aria-hidden, leaving AT users with bare CJK and abbreviated numbers.

confirmedFindings.title: textScale is offered as the core a11y lever but no acceptance/QA criterion tests that the dense multi-screen layout reflows at large text scale, and the display type scale uses `vw` units that bypass the root-font scaling model
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.11 (line 4259); docs/ui-design.md §8 (line 494) & §3.3 (line 161); docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md (line 157)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.11 (line 4259): 'Scalable text (a `textScale` setting on `GameState.settings`, applied via CSS custom property; respects the browser/root font size).' But the 'states that break layouts' checklists never include large textScale: ui-design §8 (line 494) lists 'K/M/B growth (test 999B), long logs, many revealed tabs, and the smallest viewport'; qa-playtesting line 157 lists 'K/M/B number growth ..., long event logs, many revealed tabs, empty vs full panels, the smallest mobile viewport' — neither tests the layout at 150-200% textScale, the exact stress that breaks the dense four-pillar panel + tabular K/M/B columns + many-tab nav. Worse, the type scale itself partially defeats the setting: §3.3 (line 161) `--fs-display: clamp(1.6rem, 4vw, 2.6rem)` uses a `vw` preferred term, which is viewport-relative and does NOT scale with the user's textScale/root-font in its mid band — contradicting '(respects the browser/root font size)'.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Scalable text is a locked a11y basic (D16). Offering the setting while never verifying the layout survives it — and using viewport-unit font sizing that ignores it — means the feature can ship broken (clipped tables, overlapping tabs) without any test catching it.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add 'maximum textScale (e.g. 150-200%) on the densest T2 screens' to the layout-break checklists in ui-design §8 and qa-playtesting, and reconcile the §3.3 `vw`-based display clamp with the 'respects root font size' promise (use rem-relative terms so display headings honour textScale).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Both component claims verified verbatim against source. (1) §6.11 (prd.md ~line 4259) promises textScale "respects the browser/root font size"; the three layout-break checklists (ui-design §3.3 ~165 & §8 ~494, qa-playtesting ~157) enumerate K/M/B growth, long logs, many tabs, empty/full panels, smallest viewport — none test the layout at a large textScale, the exact stress on the dense four-pillar + tabular K/M/B + many-tab UI. (2) ui-design §3.3 (~line 161) `--fs-display: clamp(1.6rem, 4vw, 2.6rem)`: min/max are rem (honour root scaling) but the preferred mid-band term is viewport-relative `4vw`, which does NOT scale with textScale — so the display heading only partially honours the "respects root font size" promise. Genuine a11y gap tied to locked basic D16. needs-human because the vw fluid-headline sizing is an intentional design choice and the textScale-max QA threshold is a judgement call.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Two reconciliations for the locked scalable-text a11y basic (D16): (a) Should the "states that break layouts" QA checklists (ui-design §3.3 & §8, qa-playtesting) add a large-textScale reflow case (e.g. 150-200% on the densest T2 screens: four-pillar Influence panel, tabular K/M/B columns, many-tab nav) so the feature can't ship with clipped tables / overlapping tabs uncaught? (b) Should §3.3's `--fs-display: clamp(1.6rem, 4vw, 2.6rem)` drop or rem-anchor its `vw` middle term so display headings honour textScale in their mid band — trading away the intentional fluid/viewport-relative headline sizing for consistency with the "respects root font size" promise?</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Should the layout-break QA checklist add a large-textScale reflow case, and should the §3.3 display font-size drop its `vw` term so headings actually scale with the textScale setting (currently the clamp's mid band is viewport-relative)?

confirmedFindings.title: Single autosave + import/'fresh start' overwrite the one slot with no required confirm and no pre-import backup — real save-loss/accidental-overwrite anxiety over a no-reset ~28.5h saga
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.8 (lines 4164, 4169, 4179-4181); docs/ui-design.md §5.10 (line 407)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.8 (line 4164): 'A single object store holds one autosave slot (canon: single autosave).' Import is specified as '(line 4169) The same serialized save is base64-encoded ... and import — the player's portable backup ... Import validates + migrates.' — i.e. import overwrites the single live slot with no stated confirm and no automatic pre-import backup. The graceful-degrade path (lines 4179-4181) offers, on a corrupt save, 're-import or a fresh start' — and 'fresh start' irreversibly discards the only slot; the 'pre-migration backup of the raw bytes' protects a failed MIGRATION, not an accidental import or fresh-start. ui-design §5.10 (line 407) mentions only a generic 'save/load confirm', not a specific destructive-overwrite confirmation. With pillar 1 'no reset, ever' and a locked ~28.5h saga, the only safety net (export) is manual and player-initiated.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: One autosave + no slots + a multi-tens-of-hours, never-resettable run makes any accidental import or 'fresh start' a catastrophic, unrecoverable loss. The export safety net only helps players who proactively exported.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Require an explicit confirm on import and on 'fresh start', auto-snapshot the current save to the raw-backup before import/fresh-start (not just before migration), and consider surfacing an export prompt at major milestones.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Quoted text confirmed verbatim. §6.8 (line 4164) locks one autosave slot; import (4168-4169) "validates + migrates" with no stated confirm; the corrupt-save recovery (4179-4181) offers "re-import or a fresh start," and the only backup mentioned is the pre-MIGRATION raw-bytes backup — which, as claimed, protects a failed migration, not an accidental import or fresh-start. ui-design §5.10 (407) lists only a generic "save/load confirm," not a destructive-overwrite confirm. Combined with locked canon "no reset, ever" and a ~28.5h saga, the single live slot can be silently overwritten with only a manual, player-initiated export as a safety net. The single-autosave decision is locked (D-013), but this finding is not disputing the slot count — it flags the missing confirm + missing pre-overwrite backup, which the PRD genuinely does not address anywhere. Not a clerical fix: adding confirms/auto-backups touches the "calm, sparse" UI language and the single-slot persistence model, so it carries real UX trade-offs → needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: On a no-reset ~28.5h saga with a single autosave slot, should the PRD require (a) an explicit destructive-action confirm on import and on "fresh start," and (b) an automatic pre-overwrite snapshot of the current slot (extending the existing pre-migration raw backup to also fire before import/fresh-start)? Trade-off: this hardens against catastrophic, unrecoverable accidental overwrite, but adds modal friction to the deliberately calm/sparse UI and extra persistence bookkeeping. Optionally also surface an export prompt at major milestones so the manual safety net isn't purely player-initiated.

confirmedFindings.title: §2.21(c) settings data shapes are out of sync with §6.4 — field-name drift (colorblindMode/reduceMotion vs colourblindMode/reducedMotion), a persisted `paused`, and ambient/sfx volume fields that exceed the locked 'mute toggle'
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.21(c) line 1667: '`A11ySettings { textScale, colorblindMode, reduceMotion, paused }` (persisted)' and line 1668: '`AudioSettings { ambientVolume, sfxVolume, muted }`'. §6.4 line 4065 stores: '`settings: { textScale: number; colourblindMode: boolean; reducedMotion: boolean; muted: boolean }`'. §6.11 uses `colourblindMode` (line 4262) and `reducedMotion` (line 4265). As literal TS field names these collide: `colorblindMode` vs `colourblindMode`, `reduceMotion` vs `reducedMotion`. §6.4 also has no home for `paused` (a transient runtime flag listed as persisted in §2.21c) nor for the separate `ambientVolume`/`sfxVolume` — and D16/F-block lock audio to 'a mute toggle' only, so the independent volume sliders are over-scope versus the single `muted` boolean §6.4 actually stores.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Two different spellings for one stored field is a silent build bug (a setting reads/writes the wrong key); and persisting `paused` would reload the player into a frozen game. The volume-slider mismatch is a scope question against the locked mute-only audio.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Align §2.21(c) field names to §6.4/§6.11 (the trivial fix below), decide whether `paused` is persisted (likely not — drop it from the persisted set), and reconcile ambient/sfx volumes against the locked 'mute toggle'.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Quoted text confirmed verbatim. §2.21(c) line 1667 reads `A11ySettings { textScale, colorblindMode, reduceMotion, paused }` (persisted) and line 1668 `AudioSettings { ambientVolume, sfxVolume, muted }`, while the authoritative TS in §6.4 line 4065 stores `settings: { textScale: number; colourblindMode: boolean; reducedMotion: boolean; muted: boolean }` and §6.11 (4262/4265) uses `colourblindMode`/`reducedMotion`. So the spelling drift (colorblindMode vs colourblindMode; reduceMotion vs reducedMotion) and the absence of any home for `paused` or for `ambientVolume`/`sfxVolume` are all genuine internal inconsistencies. The audio-canon mismatch also holds: §2.21(a) line 1659 and locked-decisions line 188 lock audio to a 'mute toggle', yet the (c) sketch lists independent volume fields (the finding miscites D-016, which is actually §4 balance, but the mute-only lock is real in canon §H/locked decisions). Not trivial-apply: the bundle mixes a clerical spelling fix with two design judgements — whether `paused` should be persisted (persisting it would reload into a frozen game, but resuming-paused could be intentional) and whether volume sliders are in scope versus the locked mute-only audio. The proposed fix edits only the A11ySettings line and never reconciles the AudioSettings volume fields, so it is incomplete. These trade-offs need a human call.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">§2.21(c)'s 'Rough DATA shape' sketch is out of sync with the authoritative §6.4/§6.11 settings shape on three points. (1) Field spelling: align the sketch to the locked spellings `colourblindMode` and `reducedMotion` (clerical — no objection). (2) Persisted `paused`: §2.21(c) lists `paused` in the persisted A11ySettings, but §6.4's stored `settings` has no `paused` field and persisting it would reload the player into a frozen game. Should `paused` be dropped from the persisted set (treated as a transient runtime flag), or is resume-while-paused intended (then §6.4 must add it)? (3) Audio scope: §2.21(a) canon and the locked decisions lock audio to a single 'mute toggle', and §6.4 stores only `muted`, yet §2.21(c) sketches independent `ambientVolume`/`sfxVolume` sliders. Should the sketch be reduced to `{ muted }` to match the locked mute-only scope, or is the audio scope being widened to per-channel volumes (which would require updating canon §H and §6.4)? Recommended default: drop `paused` from persisted, collapse AudioSettings to `{ muted }`, and fix the spellings — but (2) and (3) are scope/UX calls for the human.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.new_string: - `A11ySettings { textScale, colourblindMode, reducedMotion }` (persisted; `paused` is a transient runtime flag, not a persisted setting).

confirmedFindings.title: The v1-ending cliff-hanger is doubly-defined: §5 T3 calls the buildable stub the Porter-Guild first-contact, but that is a T2-G0/G1 beat — every other section makes the stub the castle-town
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §5 T3 header line 3653-3656 vs §3.6 G0/G1 (lines 2021-2022), G7 diegetic (line 2028), §3.5 (line 1967), §3.7.1 C0 (line 2142), §5 T3.2 beat 1 (line 3672)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §5 T3 (3653): "The single buildable T3 node for v1 is the **Kaidō Porters' & Transport Guild first-contact** (Kanta's favour run + the first *sekisho* turn-back)... The T2 capstone opens onto exactly this one teaser, then ends." But those beats are already spent IN T2: §3.6 G0 (2021) "friend **Kanta** runs the house's first consignment off the books"; §3.6 G1 (2022) "first *sekisho* turn-back → obtain a pass". And the actual T2 capstone opens elsewhere: §3.6 G7 (2028) "The page turns onto stone walls — and the story pauses here"; §3.5 (1967) "A page opens onto stone walls and a magistrate's seal — and then the story pauses"; §3.7.1 C0 (2142) "The **Castle-town map / screen** proper (beyond the T2 stub)"; §5 T3.2 beat 1 (3672) "present the Kurosawa's representative at the **Daikan's Office**."
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The single most load-bearing emotional moment of the whole release is the final beat — the cliff-hanger that decides whether v1 lands as a satisfying 'to be continued.' As written, a builder reading the §5 T3 header would ship a stub that RE-RUNS content the player already did at the START of T2 (Kanta's favour + the sekisho turn-back), and that 'pays off the MC's own porter past' which T2 already paid off (G0 + the O2/Denbei origin beats). That reads as a confused rerun, not a fresh frontier — a frustrating cut. Every other section (G7's own diegetic line, the nav-reveal, the forward C0 rung, the T3.2 beats) instead opens onto the castle-town/Daikan, which IS a clean new-frontier teaser.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide and unify what v1's stub ships. The doc-weight is overwhelming that the cliff-hanger is the castle-town/Daikan first-contact (G7 'stone walls and a magistrate's seal' → §5 T3.2 'the summons' → C0). Re-author the §5 T3 header to describe the castle-town/Daikan stub and delete the porter-guild framing (the porter-guild first-contact now lives at T2-G0/G1 and should not be re-presented as the T3 teaser).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:

The §5 T3 header (docs/prd.md:3652-3656) is titled "T3 — Castle-town (STUB in v1)" yet defines "The single buildable T3 node for v1" as the "Kaidō Porters' & Transport Guild first-contact (Kanta's favour run + the first sekisho turn-back)." Those two beats are already the T2 reveals: §3.6 G0 (2021) "friend Kanta runs the house's first consignment off the books... the Kaidō Porters' & Transport Guild seed" and §3.6 G1 (2022) "first sekisho turn-back → obtain a pass." Not a clerical fix: deciding what v1 ships and re-authoring the header rationale is a story/design judgement, so needs-human.</verdict_reason>
